title: Living in Boulder Vlog 10 -  2 -  2018: US Law Code still

enrichment.summary: Kyle describes his week-long project of building the entire US Law Code onto his history book website in a scrollable, text-to-speech-friendly format, explaining his incremental methodology for tackling its 53 titles. He shares his motivations — building 'street cred' after being demonetized by Google, wanting to be the only person offering the law code in this accessible format — and riffs on his mom's skepticism, his fiance's estimate of how long it will take, and future plans including economic Enlightenment books and a triathlon.
enrichment.speaker: kyle

stances.position: worth doing; original because no one else offers it in a continuous scrollable/text-to-speech format
not a priority; focused on street cred since he's demonetized and independently covers basic living expenses
books should be revisited; just knowing what's in a book is a huge knowledge gain even without memorization
dismisses it; actually wants nobody to care so he won't get poached or made redundant
plans to publicly pressure bankers into redistributing wealth via a jersey-and-confrontation scheme
millennials are smarter than baby boomers

claims.subject: Kyle
claims.claim: I put the US law code on my website under the history book section because I got super obsessed with Rome.

claims.subject: Kyle
claims.claim: I tried to artificially make myself obsessed with Rome to delay being obsessed with my own country.

claims.subject: US law code
claims.claim: The US law code has a reputation of having 23,000 pages, which is much larger than the Bible.

claims.subject: US tax code
claims.claim: The tax code has a reputation of having 74,000 pages, which seems impossible if it's part of the law code.

claims.subject: Kyle
claims.claim: I've been having problems with my computer freezing while working on the law code.

claims.subject: Kyle
claims.claim: I turned my computer around to avoid looking at it when it froze and started working on another computer.

claims.subject: Kyle
claims.claim: I can't build the entire US law code at once because my endurance limits me to working on it for about a month before burning out.

claims.subject: Kyle
claims.claim: I'm breaking the law code into smaller bits, like the Federalist Papers, to manage the workload.

claims.subject: Federalist Papers
claims.claim: The Federalist Papers by Alexander Hamilton, James Madison, and John Jay back up the Constitution and cite ancient Rome.

claims.subject: Federalist Papers authors
claims.claim: The authors of the Federalist Papers were probably the best politicians ever because they were charged with writing a constitution.

claims.subject: US law code
claims.claim: The US law code has 53 different titles, some of which are larger than others.

claims.subject: Kyle
claims.claim: I got bored with building the law code start to finish and started working on it in a non-linear way.

claims.subject: Kyle
claims.claim: I'm working on the law code to score street cred since I can't make money due to being demonetized by Google.

claims.subject: Kyle
claims.claim: I'm independently wealthy enough to survive on beans and rice, which keeps me on my toes.

claims.subject: Kyle
claims.claim: I want to change the world by scoring street cred instead of making money.

claims.subject: Kyle
claims.claim: I aim to become a ruthless, Wolverine-like monster by working on my projects for years.

claims.subject: Kyle
claims.claim: I plan to go after bankers by making them give me and others $50,000 a year.

claims.subject: Kyle
claims.claim: I want to buy an eternity pool for $10,000 in two and a half years.

claims.subject: Kyle
claims.claim: I'm doing the law code because it's original and nobody else has done it.

claims.subject: US law code
claims.claim: The government and Cornell's versions of the law code are the same but broken into different pieces.

claims.subject: Kyle
claims.claim: I want to make the US law code scrollable and readable on devices like iPhones.

claims.subject: Kyle
claims.claim: I'll be the only person in the world with a US law code that can be read aloud all the way through.

claims.subject: Kyle
claims.claim: The law code is interesting to zone out on, like during a long ride.

claims.subject: Kyle
claims.claim: Reading the law code once will help you know what's in it and where to find things later.

claims.subject: Kyle
claims.claim: The law code is super time-consuming because it's so big and has corrupted my computer.

claims.subject: Kyle
claims.claim: I plan to do the Articles of Confederation and books from the economic enlightenment era next.

claims.subject: Kyle
claims.claim: I want to finish projects like Googler Thing Boulder and Googler Thing Rome.

claims.subject: US law code
claims.claim: The law code is the DNA of America, representing our collective decisions and ideas.

claims.subject: Kyle
claims.claim: I'll provide a download link for the law code so others can edit it for their own use.

claims.subject: Kyle
claims.claim: I plan to do dry news updates about bills and include them in my law code.

claims.subject: Kyle
claims.claim: I want to get people interested in the law code so they can use it for political or personal reasons.

claims.subject: Kyle
claims.claim: I estimate it will take me three months to complete the law code, while my fiancée thinks it would take a normal person nine months.

claims.subject: Kyle
claims.claim: My mom thinks I'll never finish the law code and that it's useless, but I don't care because I want nobody to care.

claims.subject: Kyle
claims.claim: I don't want too many people to watch my videos because I fear my ideas might get poached.

claims.subject: Kyle
claims.claim: Once I finish the law code, I'll have new ideas that springboard off it, making me feel like the most creative person ever.

claims.subject: Kyle
claims.claim: Millennials are smarter than baby boomers, as seen in their food choices like avocado on bread instead of butter.
